---
title: "Get Started"
subtitle: "Install *Gribouille* and build a real plot, one layer at a time."
page-navigation: false
toc: true
engine: markdown
---

## Prerequisites

- [Typst](https://typst.app) {{< var typst.series >}} or later.
- Familiarity with Typst `#import` syntax.
  No prior experience with the grammar of graphics is assumed, but if you have used [`ggplot2`](https://ggplot2.tidyverse.org) in R or [`plotnine`](https://plotnine.org) in Python the mental model will feel familiar.

## Installation

Until _Gribouille_ is published on Typst Universe, import it directly from a local checkout or a Git tag.

### From Typst Universe _(once published)_

```typst
#import "@preview/gribouille:{{< env VERSION >}}": *
```

### From a local clone

```typst
#import "./path/to/gribouille/lib.typ": *
```

This imports every public symbol so you can call `plot`, `aes`, `geom-point`, and friends directly.

## Tutorial: build a penguins plot, one layer at a time

The library ships the [Palmer Penguins](https://allisonhorst.github.io/palmerpenguins/) dataset as the `penguins` symbol.
We will build a single figure end to end, adding one grammar concept per step so you can see the role each piece plays.

### 1. Data and aesthetics

Every plot starts with a dataset and a [`aes`](../reference/core/aes.qmd) mapping that binds column names to visual channels.
At minimum [`geom-point`](../reference/geoms/geom-point.qmd) needs `x` and `y`.

::: {layout-ncol="2" layout-valign="center"}

```typst
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "flipper-len", y: "body-mass"),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt),),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

```{typst}
//| output-filename: "step-1.svg"
//| alt: "Scatter of penguin body mass against flipper length, all points the same colour."
#import "./lib.typ": *

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "flipper-len", y: "body-mass"),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt),),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

:::

### 2. Encode a third variable through colour

Add `colour: "species"` to the mapping and the points split visually by group.
Every aesthetic mapping is independent of the geom, so the same colour scale will apply to whatever layers you stack on top.

::: {layout-ncol="2" layout-valign="center"}

```typst
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
  ),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.85),),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

```{typst}
//| output-filename: "step-2.svg"
//| alt: "Scatter of body mass against flipper length, points coloured by species."
#import "./lib.typ": *

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
  ),
  layers: (geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.85),),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

:::

### 3. Stack a second layer (`geom-smooth`)

Layers stack in order.
Adding a [`geom-smooth`](../reference/geoms/geom-smooth.qmd) draws one fitted line per colour group because the `colour` aesthetic is inherited from the plot-level mapping.

::: {layout-ncol="2" layout-valign="center"}

```typst
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

```{typst}
//| output-filename: "step-3.svg"
//| alt: "Per-species scatter with linear fits and 95% confidence ribbons."
#import "./lib.typ": *

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

:::

### 4. Pick a custom colour palette

[`scale-colour-discrete`](../reference/scales/scale-colour-discrete.qmd) replaces the default palette.
A matching [`scale-fill-discrete`](../reference/scales/scale-fill-discrete.qmd) keeps the smooth ribbon's fill in sync with the points.

::: {layout-ncol="2" layout-valign="center"}

```typst
#let cb = (
  rgb("#0072B2"),  // blue
  rgb("#D55E00"),  // vermillion
  rgb("#009E73"),  // bluish green
)

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  scales: (
    scale-colour-discrete(palette: cb),
    scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),
  ),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

```{typst}
//| output-filename: "step-4.svg"
//| alt: "Same scatter as step 3 with a colour-blind-friendly palette."
#import "./lib.typ": *

#let cb = (
  rgb("#0072B2"),
  rgb("#D55E00"),
  rgb("#009E73"),
)

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  scales: (
    scale-colour-discrete(palette: cb),
    scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),
  ),
  width: 8cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

:::

### 5. Split into small multiples with `facet-wrap`

[`facet-wrap`](../reference/facets/facet-wrap.qmd) lays one panel per level of a discrete column.
The same scales apply to every panel, so the species clusters can be compared across islands.

::: {layout-ncol="2" layout-valign="center"}

```typst
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  scales: (
    scale-colour-discrete(palette: cb),
    scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),
  ),
  facet: facet-wrap("island"),
  width: 12cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

```{typst}
//| output-filename: "step-5.svg"
//| alt: "Three side-by-side panels, one per island, sharing colour and axis scales."
#import "./lib.typ": *

#let cb = (
  rgb("#0072B2"),
  rgb("#D55E00"),
  rgb("#009E73"),
)

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  scales: (
    scale-colour-discrete(palette: cb),
    scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),
  ),
  facet: facet-wrap("island"),
  width: 12cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

:::

### 6. Polish with `theme()` and `labs()`

[`theme-minimal`](../reference/themes/theme-minimal.qmd) drops most of the chrome.
[`labs`](../reference/labs/labs.qmd) sets the title block and renames every axis and legend.
The single [`theme`](../reference/themes/theme.qmd) call further tweaks the title surface.

::: {layout-ncol="2" layout-valign="center"}

```typst
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  scales: (
    scale-colour-discrete(palette: cb),
    scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),
  ),
  facet: facet-wrap("island"),
  labs: labs(
    title: "Penguin body mass scales with flipper length",
    subtitle: "Same trend on every island, even though sample sizes differ",
    x: "Flipper length (mm)",
    y: "Body mass (g)",
    colour: "Species",
    fill: "Species",
  ),
  theme: theme-minimal(
    plot-title: element-text(size: 12pt, weight: "bold")
  ),
  width: 12cm,
  height: 6cm,
)
```

```{typst}
//| output-filename: "step-6.svg"
//| alt: "Polished version of the faceted scatter with title block, axis labels, and minimal theme."
#import "./lib.typ": *

#let cb = (
  rgb("#0072B2"),
  rgb("#D55E00"),
  rgb("#009E73"),
)

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(
    x: "flipper-len",
    y: "body-mass",
    colour: "species",
    fill: "species",
  ),
  layers: (
    geom-point(size: 2pt, alpha: 0.6),
    geom-smooth(method: "lm", alpha: 0.2),
  ),
  scales: (
    scale-colour-discrete(palette: cb),
    scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),
  ),
  facet: facet-wrap("island"),
  labs: labs(
    title: "Penguin body mass scales with flipper length",
    subtitle: "Same trend on every island, even though sample sizes differ",
    x: "Flipper length (mm)",
    y: "Body mass (g)",
    colour: "Species",
    fill: "Species",
  ),
  theme: theme-minimal(
    plot-title: element-text(size: 12pt, weight: "bold")
  ),
  width: 12cm,
  height: 6cm,
)
```

:::

### 7. Stats and positions: a different geom on the same data

A geom is not always one mark per row.
[`geom-bar`](../reference/geoms/geom-bar.qmd) ships with `stat: "count"` baked in, so it derives `y` from the row count.
The `position: "dodge"` parameter spreads the species side by side instead of stacking.

::: {layout-ncol="2" layout-valign="center"}

```typst
#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "island", fill: "species"),
  layers: (geom-bar(position: "dodge"),),
  scales: (scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),),
  labs: labs(x: "Island", y: "Count", fill: "Species"),
  theme: theme-minimal(),
  width: 10cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

```{typst}
//| output-filename: "step-7.svg"
//| alt: "Dodged bars showing how many penguins of each species live on each island."
#import "./lib.typ": *

#let cb = (
  rgb("#0072B2"),
  rgb("#D55E00"),
  rgb("#009E73"),
)

#plot(
  data: penguins,
  mapping: aes(x: "island", fill: "species"),
  layers: (geom-bar(position: "dodge"),),
  scales: (scale-fill-discrete(palette: cb),),
  labs: labs(x: "Island", y: "Count", fill: "Species"),
  theme: theme-minimal(),
  width: 10cm,
  height: 5cm,
)
```

:::

## The grammar in one diagram

Every plot you build composes the same pieces.
Read the chain from data on the left to the rendered figure on the right.

```{mermaid}
flowchart LR
  data["data"] --> aes["aes()"]
  aes --> layer["layer<br/>(geom + stat + position)"]
  layer --> scale["scale-*"]
  scale --> coord["coord-*"]
  coord --> facet["facet-*"]
  facet --> theme["theme + labs"]
  theme --> plot["plot()"]
```

## Next steps

- Read the [Reference](../reference/index.qmd) to see every function the library exposes.
- Browse the [Examples](../examples/index.qmd) gallery for idiomatic usage.
- Keep an eye on the [Changelog](../changelog.qmd) for API changes during the development phase.
